mnemonics: **C**auchy **C**onverges **C**ompletely: A metric space is complete if and only if every Cauchy sequence converges to a point *within* the space.
keyPoints: A complete metric space is one where every Cauchy sequence has a limit that is also in the space.
Completeness is a property of the metric space itself, not just the sequences within it.
All compact metric spaces are complete, but not all complete spaces are compact.
The real numbers R with the usual metric are the canonical example of a complete metric space.
A closed subset of a complete metric space is also complete.
commonMistakes: Assuming a Cauchy sequence must converge; this is only true in a *complete* space.
Confusing completeness with compactness or closure; they are related but distinct properties.
Forgetting that the limit of a Cauchy sequence must be in the space itself for it to be complete.
